mappings.rationale: Molnar covers the theory of plate tectonics, continental drift, seafloor spreading, and lithospheric plate boundaries—the unifying framework described in this section.
Zalasiewicz covers the origin and development of the Earth's crust, continental formation, and the geological processes of mountain building central to this section.
Redfern provides a comprehensive treatment of the Earth's origin, the development of its lithosphere, atmosphere, and hydrosphere—the core narrative of this section.
Lenton discusses the co-evolution of the Earth's envelopes—lithosphere, atmosphere, hydrosphere, and biosphere—which is the overarching theme of this section.
Palmer covers the composition and evolution of the atmosphere, relevant to this section's treatment of the origin and development of the terrestrial atmosphere.
Maslin discusses human effects on the atmosphere including acid rain, ozone depletion, and greenhouse warming described in this section.
Lowrie covers the geophysical processes underlying continental formation, mountain building, and the deep-seated Earth processes driving tectonic activity.
Volcanism is central to the formation of the lithosphere and the growth of mountain ranges at plate boundaries, as discussed in this section.
Stow covers the origin and evolution of ocean basins, relevant to this section's treatment of the origin of the hydrosphere and early oceans.
Rothery covers planetary formation and the development of planetary atmospheres, relevant to this section's discussion of the Earth's origin and atmospheric evolution.
Pierrehumbert covers planetary formation and atmospheric development, relevant to the origin of the Earth and the relation of its atmosphere to other planetary atmospheres.
Catling covers the development of the Earth's atmosphere and the co-evolution of life and atmosphere, relevant to this section's discussion of atmospheric evolution.
Goudie and Viles cover the processes of continental growth and mountain range formation from a geomorphological perspective.
Ellis discusses human effects on Earth's envelopes including acid rain, ozone depletion, and greenhouse warming, directly addressed in this section.
Dobson addresses policy responses to atmospheric degradation including ozone depletion and greenhouse warming discussed in this section.
Ali discusses sustainable responses to human impacts on the atmosphere and hydrosphere, relevant to this section's coverage of anthropogenic effects on Earth's envelopes.
Price covers the formation and growth of mountain ranges and belts, corresponding to this section's treatment of orogeny and tectonic mountain building.
Finney covers the properties and chemistry of water, relevant to the section's discussion of the origin and chemical evolution of the hydrosphere.
Coles provides context for the origin of the Earth within the broader cosmological framework, relevant to theories of the Earth's origin.
